landing.heroTitle: Don't let them take what you earned
landing.heroSubtitle: Generate legal waivers in Spanish, from your phone, in 3 minutes. State-compliant. No lawyer, no hassle.
landing.videoQuote: I used to lose days waiting for my contractor to send me the waiver. Now I generate it myself in 3 minutes and I get paid faster.
landing.testimonialsSubtitle: Subcontractors like you are already protecting their payments with ObraKit
landing.testimonial1Quote: The GC used to take weeks to pay me because I didn't have my paperwork ready. Now I send the signed waiver in 5 minutes and get paid the next day.
landing.testimonial2Quote: The best part is it's in Spanish. I don't have to ask someone to translate documents anymore. I do everything myself from my phone.
landing.testimonial3Quote: The AI warned me I was signing an unconditional waiver without having received the check. It saved me from losing my rights on a $45,000 project.
landing.howItWorksSubtitle: Three simple steps to protect your right to get paid
landing.step1Description: Project, contractor, and amount. One minute and you're set up.
landing.step2Description: Checks amounts, dates, and waiver type. If something's off, it warns you before you sign.
landing.step3Description: Sign from your phone, send the PDF to the contractor, and get paid faster.
landing.featuresTitle: Everything you need to protect your payment
landing.featuresSubtitle: Tools designed for subcontractors who want to get paid fairly
landing.feature1Description: Official statutory forms for California and Georgia. Approved templates for Texas, Florida, and New York.
landing.feature2Description: Reviews amounts, dates, and waiver type before you sign. Alerts you if something doesn't add up.
landing.feature3Description: Sign from your phone or computer. Your signature is included directly in the PDF.
landing.feature4Description: Send the waiver with PDF attachment to the contractor's email. Without leaving the app.
landing.feature5Description: The entire platform in Spanish with the option to switch to English. As it should be.
landing.feature6Description: Private storage with temporary links. No one else can see your documents.
landing.statesComingSoon: Your state isn't listed? We'll be adding more soon.
landing.pricingSubtitle: Start free, upgrade when you're ready
landing.pricingValueFrame: A mistake on a lien waiver can cost you thousands of dollars in a legal dispute. ObraKit Pro costs less than a lunch per week.
landing.faq1Answer: A lien waiver is a legal document where you waive your lien rights (the right to place a claim on the property) in exchange for payment. It's common in construction: the general contractor asks for one before paying you, to ensure you won't place a lien on the property.
landing.faq2Answer: Yes. In all 5 states we support (California, Georgia, Texas, Florida, and New York), digital waivers with electronic signatures are legally valid. For California and Georgia, we use the official statutory forms required by law.
landing.faq3Question: Do I need a credit card?
landing.faq3Answer: No. The free plan doesn't require a credit card. You can generate up to 3 waivers per month at no cost. You only need an email address to sign up.
landing.faq4Answer: We currently support California, Georgia, Texas, Florida, and New York. For California and Georgia, we generate official statutory forms. For Texas, Florida, and New York, we use professional templates that follow each state's standard practices.
landing.faq5Answer: The AI validates your waiver by checking amounts, dates, and document type, but it never blocks you. If it detects something unusual, it shows you a warning so you can decide. You always have final control over your document.
landing.faq6Answer: Yes. All your documents are stored on a private server with temporary links (signed URLs). Only you can access your waivers. We don't share your information with third parties.
landing.metaTitle: ObraKit — Professional Lien Waivers for Subcontractors
landing.metaDescription: Generate, sign, and send lien waivers that comply with your state's laws. In minutes, from any device. California, Georgia, Texas, Florida, and New York.
landing.ctaSubtitle: Generate your waiver right now and protect your right to get paid. Ready in less than 5 minutes.
landing.ctaSocialProof: Join 850+ subcontractors already protecting their payments
landing.ctaTrustNote: No credit card · Cancel anytime · Your data is secure
landing.mockupAiMessage: Amounts, dates, and waiver type verified

wizard.step3Subtitle: Review the details and validation of the waiver
wizard.throughDateHelp: The date through which this payment covers
wizard.exceptionsHelp: Items excluded from this waiver (disputes, retentions, etc.)
wizard.validationErrors: Errors found that must be fixed
wizard.signatureHint: Draw your signature with finger or mouse
wizard.signatureLandscapeHint: Rotate your phone to landscape for easier signing
wizard.scanQrCode: Scan this QR code with your phone
wizard.countyHelp: County where the property is located (for lien filing purposes)
wizard.isPublicProject: This is a public project (government-owned property)
wizard.publicProjectWarn: On public projects, you cannot file a mechanics lien against government-owned property. Your payment protection comes from payment bonds (Miller Act / Little Miller Act). A lien waiver on a public project may inadvertently waive your bond rights. Consult an attorney.
wizard.advanceWaiverBlocked: The through-date is in the future. In {state}, waivers signed before work is performed or payment is due are void and unenforceable. Change the through-date to today or an earlier date.
wizard.advanceWaiverWarn: The through-date is in the future. In {state}, waivers with future through-dates may not be enforceable. Consider using today's date or an earlier date.
wizard.disclaimerBadge: This state requires a statutory waiver form. Since we are using a general form, a legal disclaimer will be included on the document. Have it reviewed by a licensed attorney.
wizard.disclaimerBadge_es: Este estado requiere un formulario de waiver estatutario. Como estamos usando un formulario general, se incluira un aviso legal en el documento. Hagalo revisar por un abogado licenciado.
wizard.notarizationBadge: This waiver must be notarized to be enforceable in {state}. A notary acknowledgment block will be included on the document.
wizard.notarizationBadge_es: Este waiver debe ser notarizado para ser valido en {state}. Se incluira un bloque de reconocimiento notarial en el documento.

conditional_progress.name: Conditional Waiver — Progress Payment
conditional_progress.description: Use when requesting a progress payment and you have NOT yet been paid. Your lien rights are only waived IF and WHEN payment clears.
conditional_progress.risk: Low Risk — Your rights are protected until payment is received

unconditional_progress.name: Unconditional Waiver — Progress Payment
unconditional_progress.description: Use ONLY when you have already received and deposited a progress payment. This waiver takes effect IMMEDIATELY upon signing, whether or not the check clears.
unconditional_progress.risk: Medium Risk — Make sure you have payment in hand before signing

conditional_final.name: Conditional Waiver — Final Payment
conditional_final.description: Use when requesting your final payment and you have NOT yet been paid. Your rights are only waived when full payment clears.
conditional_final.risk: Low Risk — Protected until final payment is received

unconditional_final.name: Unconditional Waiver — Final Payment
unconditional_final.description: Use ONLY when you have received your complete final payment. This permanently waives ALL lien rights on the project. There is no going back.
unconditional_final.risk: High Risk — You permanently lose ALL rights. Only sign if fully paid.

stateGuides.indexMetaDescription: Complete lien waiver guides for California, Georgia, Texas, Florida, and New York. Learn your state's legal requirements.
stateGuides.indexSubtitle: Understand your state's legal requirements to generate compliant lien waivers
stateGuides.metaDescription: Complete guide to lien waivers in {stateName}. Legal requirements under {statute}, waiver types, required fields, and more.
stateGuides.heroSubtitle: Complete guide to lien waiver requirements
stateGuides.ctaTitle: Ready to create your {stateName} lien waiver?
stateGuides.ctaSubtitle: Generate your waiver in minutes with forms that comply with {stateName} law
stateGuides.CA_overview: California has the strictest lien waiver requirements in the country. State law (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 8132-8138) requires waivers to use specific statutory forms — generic templates are not permitted. Each form includes mandatory NOTICE text that must appear in type at least as large as the largest type in the form. California supports all 4 waiver types: conditional and unconditional, for both progress and final payments.
stateGuides.CA_faq1Answer: California requires specific statutory forms defined in Cal. Civ. Code §§ 8132-8138. Waivers must be "substantially" the same as the forms provided by law. ObraKit automatically generates the correct California forms.
stateGuides.CA_faq2Question: What is the mandatory NOTICE text?
stateGuides.CA_faq2Answer: Every waiver form in California must include NOTICE text that warns about the rights being waived. This text must appear in type at least as large as the largest type in the form. ObraKit includes it automatically.
stateGuides.CA_faq3Question: Are electronic signatures valid in California?
stateGuides.CA_faq3Answer: Yes. California recognizes electronic signatures under the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA) and the federal E-SIGN Act. Electronically signed waivers have the same legal validity as paper-signed ones.
stateGuides.GA_overview: Georgia requires statutory forms for lien waivers under O.C.G.A. § 44-14-366. Unlike other states, Georgia only recognizes 2 waiver types: Interim Waiver (progress payment) and Final Waiver. The Interim Waiver has a special rule: it is conditional for 90 days, then automatically converts to unconditional unless an Affidavit of Nonpayment is filed. Forms must use a minimum 12-point font size.
stateGuides.GA_faq1Answer: In Georgia, the Interim Waiver (progress payment waiver) is conditional for the first 90 days. If you don't file an Affidavit of Nonpayment within that period, the waiver automatically converts to unconditional. It's important to track payments within this timeframe.
stateGuides.GA_faq2Question: What is the minimum font size requirement?
stateGuides.GA_faq2Answer: Georgia requires lien waiver forms to use a minimum 12-point font size. ObraKit automatically generates all Georgia PDFs with the correct font size.
stateGuides.GA_faq3Answer: No. Georgia does not require lien waivers to be notarized. A valid electronic signature is sufficient for the document to be legally binding.
stateGuides.TX_overview: Texas requires statutory waiver forms under Tex. Prop. Code §§ 53.281-53.286. Waivers use an affidavit format and must be notarized to be enforceable. Texas supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit generates dedicated Texas templates with the required affidavit structure and notary acknowledgment block.
stateGuides.TX_faq1Answer: Yes. Texas requires statutory waiver forms under Tex. Prop. Code §§ 53.281-53.286. The waivers follow an affidavit format and must be notarized. ObraKit automatically generates the correct Texas forms.
stateGuides.TX_faq2Answer: Yes. Texas lien waivers must be notarized to be enforceable. ObraKit includes a notary acknowledgment block on all Texas waivers.
stateGuides.TX_faq3Question: Are electronic signatures valid in Texas?
stateGuides.TX_faq3Answer: Yes. Texas recognizes electronic signatures under TUETA. However, the notarization requirement means you will also need to visit a notary.
stateGuides.FL_overview: Florida requires statutory waiver forms under Fla. Stat. §§ 713.20, with additional compliance provisions from SB 658. Waivers must be notarized to be enforceable, with support for both physical and online notarization. Florida supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit generates dedicated Florida templates with SB 658 compliance notes and notary blocks.
stateGuides.FL_faq1Question: What makes Florida's lien waiver law different?
stateGuides.FL_faq1Answer: Florida has detailed statutory requirements under Fla. Stat. §§ 713.20 with SB 658 compliance provisions. Florida requires notarization and has strict Notice to Owner requirements and filing deadlines. ObraKit generates compliant Florida waivers with all required elements.
stateGuides.FL_faq2Answer: Yes. Florida requires lien waivers to be notarized, with support for both physical and online notarization. ObraKit includes a notary acknowledgment block on all Florida waivers.
stateGuides.FL_faq3Question: What are the important deadlines in Florida?
stateGuides.FL_faq3Answer: In Florida, subcontractors must send a Notice to Owner within the first 45 days of starting work. The lien must be filed within 90 days after the last date of work. It's important to have correct dates on your waivers.
stateGuides.NY_overview: New York does not have statutory forms for lien waivers. Under N.Y. Lien Law §§ 3-39-a, waivers are generally enforceable when voluntarily signed. New York supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit uses professional templates that follow New York's standard industry practices.
stateGuides.NY_faq1Question: Does New York require specific forms?
stateGuides.NY_faq1Answer: No. New York does not have mandatory statutory forms for lien waivers. The waiver should clearly state the rights being waived and the payment amount. ObraKit generates professional templates that meet New York industry standards.
stateGuides.NY_faq2Question: How do mechanic's liens work in New York?
stateGuides.NY_faq2Answer: In New York, subcontractors can file a mechanic's lien within 8 months after completing work (4 months for single-family homes). By signing a waiver, you give up this right to the extent specified.
stateGuides.NY_faq3Question: Are electronic signatures valid in New York?
stateGuides.NY_faq3Answer: Yes. New York recognizes electronic signatures under the New York Electronic Signatures and Records Act (ESRA). Digitally signed waivers are legally valid.
stateGuides.AZ_overview: Arizona requires statutory waiver forms under A.R.S. § 33-1008. The forms must substantially comply with the statutory templates. Notarization is not required. Advance waivers are prohibited. Arizona supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit generates dedicated Arizona templates.
stateGuides.AZ_faq1Question: Does Arizona require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.AZ_faq1Answer: Yes. Arizona requires statutory waiver forms under A.R.S. § 33-1008. ObraKit automatically generates compliant Arizona forms.
stateGuides.AZ_faq2Answer: No. Arizona does not require lien waivers to be notarized. A valid signature is sufficient.
stateGuides.AZ_faq3Question: Are advance waivers allowed in Arizona?
stateGuides.AZ_faq3Answer: No. Arizona prohibits advance waivers — waivers signed before work is performed or payment is due are void. ObraKit detects future through-dates and warns you.
stateGuides.CO_overview: Colorado does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under C.R.S. §§ 38-22-101 to 38-22-133, waivers are enforceable when properly executed. Colorado supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit uses professional templates that follow Colorado standard practices.
stateGuides.CO_faq1Answer: No. Colorado does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that meet industry standards.
stateGuides.CO_faq2Question: Are there special rules for public projects in Colorado?
stateGuides.CO_faq2Answer: Yes. On public projects (government-owned property), you cannot file a mechanic's lien. Payment bond rules apply instead. ObraKit warns you when creating waivers for public projects.
stateGuides.CO_faq3Answer: No. Colorado does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.IL_overview: Illinois does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under 770 ILCS 60/1, waivers are enforceable when properly executed. Illinois may require a Contractor's Sworn Statement alongside the waiver. Illinois supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.IL_faq1Question: Does Illinois require a sworn statement?
stateGuides.IL_faq1Answer: Illinois may require a Contractor's Sworn Statement (770 ILCS 60/2) listing all subcontractors and suppliers. This is separate from the lien waiver. Consult local counsel about your obligation.
stateGuides.IL_faq2Question: Does Illinois require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.IL_faq2Answer: No. Illinois does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that comply with Illinois practices.
stateGuides.IL_faq3Answer: No. Illinois does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.LA_overview: Louisiana does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under La. R.S. §§ 9:4801-9:4855, Louisiana uses the term 'privilege' rather than 'lien' for construction claims. Waivers are enforceable when properly executed. Louisiana supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.LA_faq1Question: What is a 'privilege' in Louisiana construction law?
stateGuides.LA_faq1Answer: Louisiana uses 'privilege' instead of 'mechanic's lien.' A privilege gives you the right to claim against the property for unpaid work. When you sign a waiver, you release this right.
stateGuides.LA_faq2Question: Does Louisiana require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.LA_faq2Answer: No. Louisiana does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that follow Louisiana standard practices.
stateGuides.LA_faq3Answer: No. Louisiana does not require lien waivers to be notarized, though notarization may add evidentiary weight.
stateGuides.MA_overview: Massachusetts does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under M.G.L. c. 254, Massachusetts distinguishes between residential and commercial projects with different lien rules. Massachusetts supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.MA_faq1Question: How does Massachusetts treat residential vs commercial projects?
stateGuides.MA_faq1Answer: Massachusetts has different lien rules for residential projects (1-4 family homes) vs commercial projects. Filing deadlines and notice requirements differ.
stateGuides.MA_faq2Question: Does Massachusetts require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.MA_faq2Answer: No. Massachusetts does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that comply with Massachusetts practices.
stateGuides.MA_faq3Answer: No. Massachusetts does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.MD_overview: Maryland does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under Md. Real Prop. §§ 9-101 to 9-113, preliminary notice is critical for preserving lien rights. Maryland supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.MD_faq1Question: What is the preliminary notice requirement in Maryland?
stateGuides.MD_faq1Answer: In Maryland, preliminary notice is critical for preserving lien rights. You must provide proper notice to the property owner before filing a lien.
stateGuides.MD_faq2Question: Does Maryland require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.MD_faq2Answer: No. Maryland does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that comply with Maryland practices.
stateGuides.MD_faq3Answer: No. Maryland does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.MI_overview: Michigan requires statutory waiver forms under MCL § 570.1115. Michigan may also require a Contractor's Sworn Statement (MCL § 570.1110) alongside lien waivers. Notarization is recommended but not required. Advance waivers are prohibited. Michigan supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit generates dedicated Michigan templates.
stateGuides.MI_faq1Question: Does Michigan require a sworn statement?
stateGuides.MI_faq1Answer: Michigan may require a Contractor's Sworn Statement (MCL § 570.1110) listing all subcontractors and suppliers. This is separate from the lien waiver. Consult local counsel.
stateGuides.MI_faq2Answer: Notarization is recommended but not legally required for Michigan lien waivers. Having the waiver notarized is considered best practice.
stateGuides.MI_faq3Question: Are advance waivers allowed in Michigan?
stateGuides.MI_faq3Answer: No. Michigan prohibits advance waivers — waivers signed before work is performed or payment is due are void. ObraKit detects future through-dates and blocks signing.
stateGuides.MN_overview: Minnesota does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under Minn. Stat. §§ 514.01-514.17, pre-lien notice requirements are important for preserving lien rights. Minnesota supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.MN_faq1Question: What is the pre-lien notice requirement in Minnesota?
stateGuides.MN_faq1Answer: Minnesota requires pre-lien notices to preserve your lien rights. Failing to provide proper notice can affect your ability to file a lien.
stateGuides.MN_faq2Question: Does Minnesota require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.MN_faq2Answer: No. Minnesota does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that comply with Minnesota practices.
stateGuides.MN_faq3Answer: No. Minnesota does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.MO_overview: Missouri requires statutory waiver forms under Mo. Rev. Stat. § 429.005. The forms must substantially conform to the prescribed templates. Missouri law may apply different rules for residential vs commercial projects. Notarization is not required. Missouri supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit generates dedicated Missouri templates.
stateGuides.MO_faq1Question: How does Missouri treat residential vs commercial projects?
stateGuides.MO_faq1Answer: Missouri law may apply different lien rules for residential vs commercial projects. ObraKit includes a note on Missouri waivers reminding you to verify which rules apply.
stateGuides.MO_faq2Question: Does Missouri require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.MO_faq2Answer: Yes. Missouri requires forms that substantially conform to Mo. Rev. Stat. § 429.005. ObraKit automatically generates compliant Missouri forms.
stateGuides.MO_faq3Answer: No. Missouri does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.MS_overview: Mississippi requires statutory waiver forms under Miss. Code § 85-7-405. Mississippi uses 'Interim' and 'Final' terminology and the forms must substantially comply with the statutory templates. Advance waivers are void. Notarization is recommended but not required. ObraKit generates dedicated Mississippi templates.
stateGuides.MS_faq1Question: What waiver types does Mississippi use?
stateGuides.MS_faq1Answer: Mississippi uses 'Interim' (progress payment) and 'Final' waiver terminology, each available as Conditional (Upon Payment) or Unconditional. Forms must comply with Miss. Code § 85-7-405.
stateGuides.MS_faq2Question: Are advance waivers allowed in Mississippi?
stateGuides.MS_faq2Answer: No. Mississippi prohibits advance waivers — waivers signed before work is performed or payment is due are void. ObraKit detects future through-dates and warns you.
stateGuides.MS_faq3Answer: Notarization is recommended but not legally required for Mississippi lien waivers. Having the waiver notarized is considered best practice.
stateGuides.NJ_overview: New Jersey does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under N.J.S.A. §§ 2A:44A-1 to 2A:44A-38, waivers are enforceable when properly executed. New Jersey supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.NJ_faq1Question: Does New Jersey require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.NJ_faq1Answer: No. New Jersey does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that comply with New Jersey practices.
stateGuides.NJ_faq2Question: How do construction liens work in New Jersey?
stateGuides.NJ_faq2Answer: In New Jersey, subcontractors can file a construction lien under N.J.S.A. §§ 2A:44A-1 to 2A:44A-38. By signing a waiver, you release this right to the extent of the payment.
stateGuides.NJ_faq3Question: Is notarization required in New Jersey?
stateGuides.NJ_faq3Answer: No. New Jersey does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.NV_overview: Nevada requires statutory waiver forms under NRS §§ 108.2453-108.2457. Nevada has a unique requirement: unconditional waivers must be notarized, while conditional waivers do not. Nevada supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit generates dedicated Nevada templates and automatically includes a notary block on unconditional waivers.
stateGuides.NV_faq1Question: When is notarization required in Nevada?
stateGuides.NV_faq1Answer: Nevada only requires notarization on unconditional waivers (both progress and final). Conditional waivers do not need notarization. ObraKit automatically includes a notary block when required.
stateGuides.NV_faq2Question: Does Nevada require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.NV_faq2Answer: Yes. Nevada requires statutory waiver forms under NRS §§ 108.2453-108.2457. ObraKit automatically generates compliant Nevada forms.
stateGuides.NV_faq3Question: Are advance waivers allowed in Nevada?
stateGuides.NV_faq3Answer: No. Nevada prohibits advance waivers. ObraKit detects future through-dates and warns you before signing.
stateGuides.OH_overview: Ohio does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under O.R.C. §§ 1311.01-1311.32, lien waivers and affidavits are treated as separate documents. Ohio supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.OH_faq1Question: How are affidavits handled in Ohio?
stateGuides.OH_faq1Answer: In Ohio, affidavits are separate from lien waivers. An affidavit may be required alongside a waiver in certain circumstances. Consult local counsel.
stateGuides.OH_faq2Question: Does Ohio require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.OH_faq2Answer: No. Ohio does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that comply with Ohio practices.
stateGuides.OH_faq3Answer: No. Ohio does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.OR_overview: Oregon does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under ORS §§ 87.001-87.093, Oregon has strict notice timelines for preserving lien rights. Oregon supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.OR_faq1Question: What are Oregon's notice timeline requirements?
stateGuides.OR_faq1Answer: Oregon has strict notice timelines for construction liens. You must provide proper notice within specific timeframes to preserve your lien rights.
stateGuides.OR_faq2Question: Does Oregon require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.OR_faq2Answer: No. Oregon does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that comply with Oregon practices.
stateGuides.OR_faq3Answer: No. Oregon does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.PA_overview: Pennsylvania does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under 49 P.S. §§ 1101-1510, Pennsylvania distinguishes between lien claims and bond claims. Pennsylvania supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.PA_faq1Question: How does Pennsylvania distinguish lien and bond claims?
stateGuides.PA_faq1Answer: Pennsylvania treats lien claims (against property) and bond claims (against payment bond) differently. Be careful about whether you are waiving lien rights only or also bond rights.
stateGuides.PA_faq2Question: Does Pennsylvania require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.PA_faq2Answer: No. Pennsylvania does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that comply with Pennsylvania practices.
stateGuides.PA_faq3Answer: No. Pennsylvania does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.UT_overview: Utah requires statutory waiver forms under Utah Code § 38-1a-802. Utah has a State Construction Registry (SCR) that may interact with waiver and lien requirements. Advance waivers are restricted. Notarization is not required. Utah supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit generates dedicated Utah templates with SCR filing reminders.
stateGuides.UT_faq1Question: What is the Utah State Construction Registry (SCR)?
stateGuides.UT_faq1Answer: The Utah SCR is a filing system that may interact with your lien waiver and lien rights. ObraKit includes a reminder to verify your SCR filing status on all Utah waivers.
stateGuides.UT_faq2Question: Are advance waivers allowed in Utah?
stateGuides.UT_faq2Answer: No. Utah restricts advance waivers — waivers signed before work is performed or payment is due may not be enforceable. ObraKit detects future through-dates and warns you.
stateGuides.UT_faq3Answer: No. Utah does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.WA_overview: Washington does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under RCW §§ 60.04.011-60.04.904, Washington has strict retainage rules that affect payment and waiver practices. Washington supports all 4 waiver types.
stateGuides.WA_faq1Answer: Washington has strict retainage rules governing how much of each payment can be held back. Account for retainage when reviewing waiver amounts.
stateGuides.WA_faq2Question: Does Washington require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.WA_faq2Answer: No. Washington does not mandate statutory waiver forms. ObraKit generates professional templates that comply with Washington practices.
stateGuides.WA_faq3Answer: No. Washington does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.WY_overview: Wyoming requires statutory waiver forms under Wyo. Stat. § 29-2-110. The forms must substantially comply with the statutory templates. Notarization is not required. Wyoming supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit generates dedicated Wyoming templates.
stateGuides.WY_faq1Question: Does Wyoming require specific waiver forms?
stateGuides.WY_faq1Answer: Yes. Wyoming requires statutory waiver forms under Wyo. Stat. § 29-2-110. ObraKit automatically generates compliant Wyoming forms.
stateGuides.WY_faq2Answer: No. Wyoming does not require lien waivers to be notarized. Electronic signatures are legally valid.
stateGuides.WY_faq3Question: Are electronic signatures valid in Wyoming?
stateGuides.WY_faq3Answer: Yes. Wyoming recognizes electronic signatures under UETA. Digitally signed waivers have the same legal validity as paper-signed ones.
